Inclusion in an NLM database does not imply endorsement of, or agreement with, the contents by NLM or the National Institutes of Health.  \nLearn more: [PMC Disclaimer](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/disclaimer/) | [ PMC Copyright Notice ](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\n![Biomedical Journal logo](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/banners/logo-biomedicalj.gif)\nBiomed J\n. 2023 Nov 7;47(1):100677. doi: [10.1016/j.bj.2023.100677](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bj.2023.100677)\n  * [Search in PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/search/?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[jour\\])\n  * [Search in PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[jour\\])\n  * [View in NLM Catalog](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nlmcatalog?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[Title%20Abbreviation\\])\n  * [Add to search](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[jour\\])\n\n# Impact of fasting & ketogenic interventions on the NLRP3 inflammasome: A narrative review\n[Helena Neudorf](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Neudorf%20H\"\\[Author\\])\n### Helena Neudorf\n1University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada\nFind articles by [Helena Neudorf](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Neudorf%20H\"\\[Author\\])\n1, [Jonathan P Little](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Little%20JP\"\\[Author\\])\n### Jonathan P Little\n1University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada\nFind articles by [Jonathan P Little](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Little%20JP\"\\[Author\\])\n1,\u2217\n  * Author information\n  * Article notes\n  * Copyright and License information\n\n1University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada\n\u2217\nCorresponding author. University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada. jonathan.little@ubc.ca\nReceived 2023 Jun 29; Revised 2023 Oct 27; Accepted 2023 Oct 30; Issue date 2024 Feb.\n\u00a9 2023 The Authors\nThis is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).\n[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\nPMCID: PMC10821592 PMID: [37940045](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37940045/)\n##  Abstract\nOveractivation of the NLRP3 inflammasome is implicated in chronic low-grade inflammation associated with various disease states, including obesity, type 2 diabetes, atherosclerosis, Alzheimer's disease, and Parkinson's disease. Emerging evidence, mostly from cell and animal models of disease, supports a role for ketosis in general, and the main circulating ketone body beta-hydroxybutyrate (BHB) in particular, in reducing NLRP3 inflammasome activation to improve chronic inflammation. As a result, interventions that can induce ketosis (e.g., fasting, intermittent fasting, time-restricted feeding/eating, very low-carbohydrate high-fat ketogenic diets) and/or increase circulating BHB (e.g., exogenous ketone supplementation) have garnered increasing interest for their therapeutic potential. The purpose of the present review is to summarize our current understanding of the literature on how ketogenic interventions impact the NLRP3 inflammasome across human, rodent and cell models. Overall, there is convincing evidence that ketogenic interventions, likely acting through multiple interacting mechanisms in a cell-, disease- and context-specific manner, can reduce NLRP3 inflammasome activation. The evidence supports a direct effect of BHB, although it is important to consider the myriad of other metabolic responses to fasting or ketogenic diet interventions (e.g., elevated lipolysis, low insulin, stable glucose, negative energy balance) that may also impact innate immune responses. Future research is needed to translate promising findings from discovery science to clinical application.\n**Keywords:** Ketogenic diets, NLRP3 inflammasome, Fasting, Beta-hydroxybutyrate, Inflammation\n##  Highlights\n  * \u2022\nFasting and ketogenic interventions suppress NLRP3 activation, likely via BHB\n  * \u2022\nBHB inhibits NLRP3 activation through multiple mechanisms\n  * \u2022\nBHB inhibits K+ efflux, ROS, and ER stress-induced NLRP3 inflammasome activation\n  * \u2022\nBHB likely acts differentially across species, diseases and physiological contexts\n\n##  1. Introduction\n### 1.1. Relevance of the NLRP3 inflammasome in health & disease\nPersistent low-grade inflammation, largely driven by overactivation of cells of the innate immune system, is now recognized to play a significant role in many chronic and aging-associated diseases, including obesity, type 2 diabetes (T2D), cardiovascular disease (CVD), and Alzheimer's disease. Understanding the cellular and molecular processes underpinning such chronic inflammatory disease states is therefore important as it may guide potential (anti-inflammatory) treatments. Indeed, such understanding has led to anti-inflammatory therapy such as interleukin (IL)-1 receptor blockade or IL-1\u03b2 antagonists, which block pro-inflammatory IL-1\u03b2 signaling, with randomized controlled trials showing slower progression of CVD and T2D using such agents [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib1),[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib2)]. The NOD-like receptor (NLR) family pyrin domain containing 3 (NLRP3) inflammasome is one molecular pathway involved in innate immune activation that has been repeatedly implicated in chronic inflammation associated with various disease states [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)]. The detailed molecular mechanisms of NLRP3 signaling and activation have been extensively described in several recent reviews [[[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib4), [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib5), [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib6), [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib7), [[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib8)] and a summary is presented in [[Fig. 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#fig1)].\n#### Fig. 1.\n[![Fig.\u00a01](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/6610/10821592/dfad6d2864d0/gr1.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10821592_gr1.jpg)\n[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/figure/fig1/)\nMechanistic overview of NLRP3 inflammasome signalling. Abbreviations:ASC: associated speck-like protein; CARD: caspase activation and recruitment domain; FADD:Fas-associated death domain protein; IKK:IkB kinase; IL: interleukin; LPS: lipopolysaccharide; MyD88: myeloid differentiation primary response 88; NACHT: NAIP (neuronal apoptosis inhibitor protein), C2TA (class 2 transcription activator, of the MHC), HET-E (heterokaryon incompatibility) and TP1 (telomerase-associated protein 1); Nek7: never in mitosis gene A (NIMA)-related kinase; NF-kB: nuclear factor k-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells; NLRP3:NOD-like receptor pyrin domain containing 3; PYD: pyrin domain; LRR: C-terminal receptor domain of leucine-rich repeats; RIPK1: receptor-interacting serine/threonine-protein kinase 1; TLR4: toll-like receptor 4; TRIF: toll-interleukin-1 receptor (TIR) domain containing adaptor inducing interferon beta.\nBriefly, a danger- or pathogen-associated molecular pattern (DAMP or PAMP) binds its ligand (e.g., toll-like receptor (TLR)) which induces activation of nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells (NF-\u03baB) and consequent transcription of NLRP3 and IL1B mRNA. In canonical NLRP3 signalling, a second activating signal is required following the initial priming signal. The second signal induces NLRP3 oligomerization and recruitment of NLRP3, associated speck-like protein (ASC) and pro-caspase-1. Pro-caspase-1 is cleaved autoproteolytically, and the resulting caspase-1 cleaves pro-IL-1\u03b2 and pro-IL-18 into their active forms, which are then secreted from the cell [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)]. It is important to acknowledge that our understanding of the mechanisms by which the NLRP3 inflammasome is activated is largely based on studies in rodents, and evidence suggests that alternative mechanisms of activation exist in human immune cells in addition to the canonical two-step model [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib9)]. Importantly, in human monocytes, a single TLR4-mediated stimulus is sufficient to activate the NLRP3 inflammasome, which can occur independently of caspase-4 and K+ efflux. This single-step model of activation, coined \u201calternative activation\u201d, induces a more moderated inflammatory response in which pyroptosis does not occur and less IL-1\u03b2 is secreted [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib9)]. A final \u201cnon-canonical\u201d mode of activation has also been identified in which rodent caspase-11 is also activated by a single TLR4-mediated stimulus and activates NLRP3 directly via K+ efflux [[[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib9), [[10]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib10), [[11]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib11), [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib12)]. Despite these multiple modes of NLRP3 inflammasome activation, the ultimate release of pro-inflammatory cytokines remains a tightly regulated process.\nNumerous studies, in both rodent and human models, have reported heightened NLRP3 activation in conditions where chronic low-grade inflammation is implicated in disease pathophysiology [[Fig. 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#fig2)]. This includes NLRP3 in adipose tissue-infiltrating macrophages, which contribute to the development of insulin resistance and low-grade inflammation in obesity [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)] and in pancreas-infiltrating macrophages, which respond to a myriad of metabolites (e.g., ceramides, saturated fatty acids, hyperglycemia), reactive oxygen species (ROS), and aggregated islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP) contributing to beta cell death/dysfunction in T2D [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3),[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib13)]. NLRP3 is also implicated in atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Macrophage NLRP3 in atherosclerotic plaques are activated by oxidized LDL, cholesterol crystals, and calcium phosphate crystals [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3),[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib14)]. Additionally, NLRP3 in microglia contribute to the pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease in response to amyloid-\u03b2 and aggregated Tau [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3),[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib15)], and microglia and astrocyte NLRP3 respond to \u03b1-synuclein via TLR2 in Parkinson's disease (PD), which is likely compounded by the loss of dopaminergic neurons which negatively regulate NLRP3 activation [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)]. In many instances, the effects observed in cells and tissues obtained from intact animals and humans can be recapitulated in cell culture models using disease-specific stimuli (e.g., IAPP for T2D, amyloid-\u03b2 for AD) suggesting that overactivation of the NLRP3 inflammasome can be specific to disease pathology. Therefore, reducing activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome is hypothesized to help lower inflammation in many chronic disease states [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)].\n#### Fig. 2.\n[![Fig.\u00a02](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/6610/10821592/76e4bea6c456/gr2.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10821592_gr2.jpg)\n[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/figure/fig2/)\nThe NLRP3 inflammasome as a central mediator of disease. AT, adipose tissue; NLRP3, NOD-like receptor pyrin domain containing 3; oxLDL, oxidized low-density lipoprotein; IAPP, islet amyloid polypeptide. Abbreviations:AT: adipose tissue; IAPP: islet amyloid polypeptide; NLRP3: NOD-like receptor pyrin domain containing 3; oxLDL: oxidized low-density lipoprotein.\nThe purpose of the present review is to summarize our current understanding of the literature on ketogenic interventions and NLRP3 across human, rodent and cell models. This review also aims to bring nuance to the broader physiological effects of ketogenic interventions and their potential effects on signalling through the NLRP3 inflammasome.\n##  2. Methods\nHere we present a narrative review with defined search criteria. All searches were conducted in the MEDLINE database. To adequately encompass the variety of names used to describe ketogenic interventions, studies were identified if they contained any of the following MeSH terms: intermittent fasting; fasting; caloric restriction; diet, carbohydrate-restricted; diet, high-protein low carbohydrate; diet, ketogenic; ketones; ketone bodies; 3-hydroxybutyric acid; or the key words \u03b2-hydroxybutyrate, high-fat low-carbohydrate diet. Studies directly quantifying NLRP3 inflammasome activation were identified if they contained any of the following MeSH terms: NLR Family, Pyrin Domain-Containing 3 Protein; Inflammasomes; or the key word NLRP3 inflammasome. Combined, these search criteria identified 102 papers. Studies were included if they were original research articles and were conducted in human or rodent cell lines, _ex vivo_ or _in vivo_ rodent models, and _ex vivo_ or _in vivo_ human models, and directly quantified NLRP3 inflammasome activation in response to ketogenic interventions in the context of non-infectious disease. Additionally, reference lists of the included papers were checked for studies that may have been missed with these search criteria. Following exclusions, 30 studies were identified as relevant by these criteria [[Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#tbl1)].\n### Table 1.\nSummary of included studies Inclusion in an NLM database does not imply endorsement of, or agreement with, the contents by NLM or the National Institutes of Health.  \nLearn more: [PMC Disclaimer](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/disclaimer/) | [ PMC Copyright Notice ](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\n![Biomedical Journal logo](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/banners/logo-biomedicalj.gif)\nBiomed J\n. 2023 Nov 7;47(1):100677. doi: [10.1016/j.bj.2023.100677](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bj.2023.100677)\n  * [Search in PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/search/?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[jour\\])\n  * [Search in PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[jour\\])\n  * [View in NLM Catalog](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nlmcatalog?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[Title%20Abbreviation\\])\n  * [Add to search](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/?term=\"Biomed%20J\"\\[jour\\])\n\n# Impact of fasting & ketogenic interventions on the NLRP3 inflammasome: A narrative review\n[Helena Neudorf](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Neudorf%20H\"\\[Author\\])\n### Helena Neudorf\n1University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada\nFind articles by [Helena Neudorf](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Neudorf%20H\"\\[Author\\])\n1, [Jonathan P Little](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Little%20JP\"\\[Author\\])\n### Jonathan P Little\n1University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada\nFind articles by [Jonathan P Little](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Little%20JP\"\\[Author\\])\n1,\u2217\n  * Author information\n  * Article notes\n  * Copyright and License information\n\n1University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada\n\u2217\nCorresponding author. University of British Columbia, Okanagan Campus, Kelowna, BC, Canada. jonathan.little@ubc.ca\nReceived 2023 Jun 29; Revised 2023 Oct 27; Accepted 2023 Oct 30; Issue date 2024 Feb.\n\u00a9 2023 The Authors\nThis is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).\n[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\nPMCID: PMC10821592 PMID: [37940045](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37940045/)\n##  Abstract\nOveractivation of the NLRP3 inflammasome is implicated in chronic low-grade inflammation associated with various disease states, including obesity, type 2 diabetes, atherosclerosis, Alzheimer's disease, and Parkinson's disease. Emerging evidence, mostly from cell and animal models of disease, supports a role for ketosis in general, and the main circulating ketone body beta-hydroxybutyrate (BHB) in particular, in reducing NLRP3 inflammasome activation to improve chronic inflammation. As a result, interventions that can induce ketosis (e.g., fasting, intermittent fasting, time-restricted feeding/eating, very low-carbohydrate high-fat ketogenic diets) and/or increase circulating BHB (e.g., exogenous ketone supplementation) have garnered increasing interest for their therapeutic potential. The purpose of the present review is to summarize our current understanding of the literature on how ketogenic interventions impact the NLRP3 inflammasome across human, rodent and cell models. Overall, there is convincing evidence that ketogenic interventions, likely acting through multiple interacting mechanisms in a cell-, disease- and context-specific manner, can reduce NLRP3 inflammasome activation. The evidence supports a direct effect of BHB, although it is important to consider the myriad of other metabolic responses to fasting or ketogenic diet interventions (e.g., elevated lipolysis, low insulin, stable glucose, negative energy balance) that may also impact innate immune responses. Future research is needed to translate promising findings from discovery science to clinical application.\n**Keywords:** Ketogenic diets, NLRP3 inflammasome, Fasting, Beta-hydroxybutyrate, Inflammation\n##  Highlights\n  * \u2022\nFasting and ketogenic interventions suppress NLRP3 activation, likely via BHB\n  * \u2022\nBHB inhibits NLRP3 activation through multiple mechanisms\n  * \u2022\nBHB inhibits K+ efflux, ROS, and ER stress-induced NLRP3 inflammasome activation\n  * \u2022\nBHB likely acts differentially across species, diseases and physiological contexts\n\n##  1. Introduction\n### 1.1. Relevance of the NLRP3 inflammasome in health & disease\nPersistent low-grade inflammation, largely driven by overactivation of cells of the innate immune system, is now recognized to play a significant role in many chronic and aging-associated diseases, including obesity, type 2 diabetes (T2D), cardiovascular disease (CVD), and Alzheimer's disease. Understanding the cellular and molecular processes underpinning such chronic inflammatory disease states is therefore important as it may guide potential (anti-inflammatory) treatments. Indeed, such understanding has led to anti-inflammatory therapy such as interleukin (IL)-1 receptor blockade or IL-1\u03b2 antagonists, which block pro-inflammatory IL-1\u03b2 signaling, with randomized controlled trials showing slower progression of CVD and T2D using such agents [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib1),[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib2)]. The NOD-like receptor (NLR) family pyrin domain containing 3 (NLRP3) inflammasome is one molecular pathway involved in innate immune activation that has been repeatedly implicated in chronic inflammation associated with various disease states [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)]. The detailed molecular mechanisms of NLRP3 signaling and activation have been extensively described in several recent reviews [[[4]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib4), [[5]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib5), [[6]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib6), [[7]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib7), [[8]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib8)] and a summary is presented in [[Fig. 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#fig1)].\n#### Fig. 1.\n[![Fig.\u00a01](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/6610/10821592/dfad6d2864d0/gr1.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10821592_gr1.jpg)\n[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/figure/fig1/)\nMechanistic overview of NLRP3 inflammasome signalling. Abbreviations:ASC: associated speck-like protein; CARD: caspase activation and recruitment domain; FADD:Fas-associated death domain protein; IKK:IkB kinase; IL: interleukin; LPS: lipopolysaccharide; MyD88: myeloid differentiation primary response 88; NACHT: NAIP (neuronal apoptosis inhibitor protein), C2TA (class 2 transcription activator, of the MHC), HET-E (heterokaryon incompatibility) and TP1 (telomerase-associated protein 1); Nek7: never in mitosis gene A (NIMA)-related kinase; NF-kB: nuclear factor k-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells; NLRP3:NOD-like receptor pyrin domain containing 3; PYD: pyrin domain; LRR: C-terminal receptor domain of leucine-rich repeats; RIPK1: receptor-interacting serine/threonine-protein kinase 1; TLR4: toll-like receptor 4; TRIF: toll-interleukin-1 receptor (TIR) domain containing adaptor inducing interferon beta.\nBriefly, a danger- or pathogen-associated molecular pattern (DAMP or PAMP) binds its ligand (e.g., toll-like receptor (TLR)) which induces activation of nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells (NF-\u03baB) and consequent transcription of NLRP3 and IL1B mRNA. In canonical NLRP3 signalling, a second activating signal is required following the initial priming signal. The second signal induces NLRP3 oligomerization and recruitment of NLRP3, associated speck-like protein (ASC) and pro-caspase-1. Pro-caspase-1 is cleaved autoproteolytically, and the resulting caspase-1 cleaves pro-IL-1\u03b2 and pro-IL-18 into their active forms, which are then secreted from the cell [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)]. It is important to acknowledge that our understanding of the mechanisms by which the NLRP3 inflammasome is activated is largely based on studies in rodents, and evidence suggests that alternative mechanisms of activation exist in human immune cells in addition to the canonical two-step model [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib9)]. Importantly, in human monocytes, a single TLR4-mediated stimulus is sufficient to activate the NLRP3 inflammasome, which can occur independently of caspase-4 and K+ efflux. This single-step model of activation, coined \u201calternative activation\u201d, induces a more moderated inflammatory response in which pyroptosis does not occur and less IL-1\u03b2 is secreted [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib9)]. A final \u201cnon-canonical\u201d mode of activation has also been identified in which rodent caspase-11 is also activated by a single TLR4-mediated stimulus and activates NLRP3 directly via K+ efflux [[[9]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib9), [[10]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib10), [[11]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib11), [[12]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib12)]. Despite these multiple modes of NLRP3 inflammasome activation, the ultimate release of pro-inflammatory cytokines remains a tightly regulated process.\nNumerous studies, in both rodent and human models, have reported heightened NLRP3 activation in conditions where chronic low-grade inflammation is implicated in disease pathophysiology [[Fig. 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#fig2)]. This includes NLRP3 in adipose tissue-infiltrating macrophages, which contribute to the development of insulin resistance and low-grade inflammation in obesity [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)] and in pancreas-infiltrating macrophages, which respond to a myriad of metabolites (e.g., ceramides, saturated fatty acids, hyperglycemia), reactive oxygen species (ROS), and aggregated islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP) contributing to beta cell death/dysfunction in T2D [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3),[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib13)]. NLRP3 is also implicated in atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Macrophage NLRP3 in atherosclerotic plaques are activated by oxidized LDL, cholesterol crystals, and calcium phosphate crystals [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3),[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib14)]. Additionally, NLRP3 in microglia contribute to the pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease in response to amyloid-\u03b2 and aggregated Tau [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3),[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib15)], and microglia and astrocyte NLRP3 respond to \u03b1-synuclein via TLR2 in Parkinson's disease (PD), which is likely compounded by the loss of dopaminergic neurons which negatively regulate NLRP3 activation [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)]. In many instances, the effects observed in cells and tissues obtained from intact animals and humans can be recapitulated in cell culture models using disease-specific stimuli (e.g., IAPP for T2D, amyloid-\u03b2 for AD) suggesting that overactivation of the NLRP3 inflammasome can be specific to disease pathology. Therefore, reducing activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome is hypothesized to help lower inflammation in many chronic disease states [[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#bib3)].\n#### Fig. 2.\n[![Fig.\u00a02](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/6610/10821592/76e4bea6c456/gr2.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=10821592_gr2.jpg)\n[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/figure/fig2/)\nThe NLRP3 inflammasome as a central mediator of disease. AT, adipose tissue; NLRP3, NOD-like receptor pyrin domain containing 3; oxLDL, oxidized low-density lipoprotein; IAPP, islet amyloid polypeptide. Abbreviations:AT: adipose tissue; IAPP: islet amyloid polypeptide; NLRP3: NOD-like receptor pyrin domain containing 3; oxLDL: oxidized low-density lipoprotein.\nThe purpose of the present review is to summarize our current understanding of the literature on ketogenic interventions and NLRP3 across human, rodent and cell models. This review also aims to bring nuance to the broader physiological effects of ketogenic interventions and their potential effects on signalling through the NLRP3 inflammasome.\n##  2. Methods\nHere we present a narrative review with defined search criteria. All searches were conducted in the MEDLINE database. To adequately encompass the variety of names used to describe ketogenic interventions, studies were identified if they contained any of the following MeSH terms: intermittent fasting; fasting; caloric restriction; diet, carbohydrate-restricted; diet, high-protein low carbohydrate; diet, ketogenic; ketones; ketone bodies; 3-hydroxybutyric acid; or the key words \u03b2-hydroxybutyrate, high-fat low-carbohydrate diet. Studies directly quantifying NLRP3 inflammasome activation were identified if they contained any of the following MeSH terms: NLR Family, Pyrin Domain-Containing 3 Protein; Inflammasomes; or the key word NLRP3 inflammasome. Combined, these search criteria identified 102 papers. Studies were included if they were original research articles and were conducted in human or rodent cell lines, _ex vivo_ or _in vivo_ rodent models, and _ex vivo_ or _in vivo_ human models, and directly quantified NLRP3 inflammasome activation in response to ketogenic interventions in the context of non-infectious disease. Additionally, reference lists of the included papers were checked for studies that may have been missed with these search criteria. Following exclusions, 30 studies were identified as relevant by these criteria [[Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10821592/#tbl1)].\n### Table 1.\nSummary of included studies .codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_verdict_iter_6.txt:178:4. **Convention dependency on Claude self-discipline:** the canonical-diff-sha256 trailer is Claude-emitted. A malicious Claude could:
.codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_verdict_iter_6.txt:179:   - Compute the trailer hash AFTER submitting to Codex but before the workflow runs (race). Mitigation: Claude commits the trailer FIRST, then `codex exec --skip-git-repo-check` reads the diff (with trailer) and emits APPROVE on it. The codex_diff.patch is committed as one atomic unit to the PR. If Claude updates the patch post-Codex-APPROVE, the verdict file's APPROVE no longer binds to it — but the workflow still passes because verdict file says APPROVE and trailer matches diff. **This is a real residual risk:** Codex must review codex_diff.patch INCLUDING its trailer line, so the APPROVE binds to BOTH the code-change-diff AND the declared sha256. If Claude post-edits the trailer to match a different actual diff, the trailer's value changes → Codex's APPROVE was for a different patch byte-for-byte → Codex's APPROVE no longer applies. Operationally: the workflow only verifies `actual_hash == declared_hash`, not that Codex APPROVE'd the specific declared hash. Phase-2 hardening would have Codex output the hash IT approved (in codex_diff_audit.txt) and the workflow cross-check. Acknowledged Phase-1 limitation; user reads `git log` morning catches forgery.

.codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_verdict_iter_5.txt:111:4. **Convention dependency on Claude self-discipline:** the canonical-diff-sha256 trailer is Claude-emitted. A malicious Claude could:
.codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_verdict_iter_5.txt:112:   - Compute the trailer hash AFTER submitting to Codex but before the workflow runs (race). Mitigation: Claude commits the trailer FIRST, then `codex exec --skip-git-repo-check` reads the diff (with trailer) and emits APPROVE on it. The codex_diff.patch is committed as one atomic unit to the PR. If Claude updates the patch post-Codex-APPROVE, the verdict file's APPROVE no longer binds to it — but the workflow still passes because verdict file says APPROVE and trailer matches diff. **This is a real residual risk:** Codex must review codex_diff.patch INCLUDING its trailer line, so the APPROVE binds to BOTH the code-change-diff AND the declared sha256. If Claude post-edits the trailer to match a different actual diff, the trailer's value changes → Codex's APPROVE was for a different patch byte-for-byte → Codex's APPROVE no longer applies. Operationally: the workflow only verifies `actual_hash == declared_hash`, not that Codex APPROVE'd the specific declared hash. Phase-2 hardening would have Codex output the hash IT approved (in codex_diff_audit.txt) and the workflow cross-check. Acknowledged Phase-1 limitation; user reads `git log` morning catches forgery.

.codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_verdict_iter_4.txt:51:   - `sha256sum` produces 64-hex; `awk '{print $1}'` extracts the hash; `tail -1` of grep gets the LAST trailer (in case there are multiple — Claude error).
.codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_verdict_iter_4.txt:57:3. **PRD3-P2-001 closure:** open `state/polaris_restart/issue_breakdown.md:89-99`. Verify the §3 5-artifact list now matches CLAUDE.md / plan.md exactly (no `decision.md`; instead `codex_diff.patch` with canonical-diff-sha256 trailer note).
.codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_verdict_iter_4.txt:59:4. **Convention dependency on Claude self-discipline:** the canonical-diff-sha256 trailer is Claude-emitted. A malicious Claude could:
.codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_verdict_iter_4.txt:60:   - Compute the trailer hash AFTER submitting to Codex but before the workflow runs (race). Mitigation: Claude commits the trailer FIRST, then `codex exec --skip-git-repo-check` reads the diff (with trailer) and emits APPROVE on it. The codex_diff.patch is committed as one atomic unit to the PR. If Claude updates the patch post-Codex-APPROVE, the verdict file's APPROVE no longer binds to it — but the workflow still passes because verdict file says APPROVE and trailer matches diff. **This is a real residual risk:** Codex must review codex_diff.patch INCLUDING its trailer line, so the APPROVE binds to BOTH the code-change-diff AND the declared sha256. If Claude post-edits the trailer to match a different actual diff, the trailer's value changes → Codex's APPROVE was for a different patch byte-for-byte → Codex's APPROVE no longer applies. Operationally: the workflow only verifies `actual_hash == declared_hash`, not that Codex APPROVE'd the specific declared hash. Phase-2 hardening would have Codex output the hash IT approved (in codex_diff_audit.txt) and the workflow cross-check. Acknowledged Phase-1 limitation; user reads `git log` morning catches forgery.

.codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_brief.md:188:  - Convention added to brief: Claude commits `# canonical-diff-sha256: <hash>` line in codex_diff.patch BEFORE submitting it to Codex. Codex APPROVE then binds to that specific hash. Any post-APPROVE code change to the PR (additions, fixes, even comment-only) changes the canonical-diff hash and forces a re-review.
.codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_brief.md:193:  - `.codex/<id>/codex_diff.patch` (with canonical-diff-sha256 trailer per PRD3-P1-001)
.codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_brief.md:202:.github/workflows/codex-required.yml             (UPDATED — diff-binding via canonical-diff-sha256 + tightened issue_id regex + fetch-depth 0)
.codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_brief.md:214:   - `sha256sum` produces 64-hex; `awk '{print $1}'` extracts the hash; `tail -1` of grep gets the LAST trailer (in case there are multiple — Claude error).
.codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_brief.md:220:3. **PRD3-P2-001 closure:** open `state/polaris_restart/issue_breakdown.md:89-99`. Verify the §3 5-artifact list now matches CLAUDE.md / plan.md exactly (no `decision.md`; instead `codex_diff.patch` with canonical-diff-sha256 trailer note).
.codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_brief.md:222:4. **Convention dependency on Claude self-discipline:** the canonical-diff-sha256 trailer is Claude-emitted. A malicious Claude could:
.codex\pr_d_mechanical_gates_review_brief.md:223:   - Compute the trailer hash AFTER submitting to Codex but before the workflow runs (race). Mitigation: Claude commits the trailer FIRST, then `codex exec --skip-git-repo-check` reads the diff (with trailer) and emits APPROVE on it. The codex_diff.patch is committed as one atomic unit to the PR. If Claude updates the patch post-Codex-APPROVE, the verdict file's APPROVE no longer binds to it — but the workflow still passes because verdict file says APPROVE and trailer matches diff. **This is a real residual risk:** Codex must review codex_diff.patch INCLUDING its trailer line, so the APPROVE binds to BOTH the code-change-diff AND the declared sha256. If Claude post-edits the trailer to match a different actual diff, the trailer's value changes → Codex's APPROVE was for a different patch byte-for-byte → Codex's APPROVE no longer applies. Operationally: the workflow only verifies `actual_hash == declared_hash`, not that Codex APPROVE'd the specific declared hash. Phase-2 hardening would have Codex output the hash IT approved (in codex_diff_audit.txt) and the workflow cross-check. Acknowledged Phase-1 limitation; user reads `git log` morning catches forgery.

def _staged_diff_sha256() -> str:
    """SHA256 of `git diff --cached` output, EXCLUDING verdict files + audit log
    (which are part of the same commit but are emitted by the gate workflow itself).

    Per Codex round-1 P0-5 fix + P1-2 chicken-and-egg resolution: verdict files at
    outputs/audits/verdicts/<task>/iter_*.json AND outputs/audits/codex_audit.jsonl
    are written DURING the gate workflow and committed atomically with substrate.
    The verdict's `diff_sha256` field covers SUBSTRATE diff only — that's the thing
    Codex actually reviewed. Excluding verdict files from the hash lets us atomic-
    commit substrate + verdict without circular-hash issues.
    """
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "diff", "--cached", "--",
         ":(exclude)outputs/audits/verdicts/**",
         ":(exclude)outputs/audits/codex_audit.jsonl",
         ":(exclude)outputs/audits/manifests/**"],
        cwd=str(POLARIS_ROOT),
        capture_output=True,
        timeout=15,
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        raise GateError(f"git diff failed: {result.stderr.decode('utf-8', errors='replace')}")
    return hashlib.sha256(result.stdout).hexdigest()

## LOC exemption requested

CHARTER §1 200-cap exceeded by 53. Drivers: full ContradictionSide schema with 6 fields + validator + 4 new tests + import/helper (backend ~66 LOC); ContradictionPane component with 6 sub-testids per side (~99 LOC); badge → button conversion + state plumbing (~35 LOC); demo 3-sides extension (~33 LOC); spec extension (~29 LOC). Splitting Sheet from schema would surface schema field unused (substrate-only) — substrate-honesty anti-pattern. Exemption analogous to I-f5-003/4 (245/246 LOC), I-f7-001 (217 LOC) — binding multi-substrate coverage in a single coherent backend+UI feature.

  }) => {

    // no golden fixture populated drop_reason. Cycle-2 audit P1.1

test.describe("WCAG 2.5.8 target-size sweep (F-28 — broader than axe)", () => {
  // axe's target-size rule has loose overlap-detection; cycle-8 P1.1 and
  // P1.4 surfaced multiple <button>/<label>/<a role="button"> surfaces
  // that pass axe but fail strict WCAG 2.2 SC 2.5.8 AA (24x24 minimum).
  // This sweep walks every clickable element + asserts ≥24x24 directly.

  test("Dashboard — all clickable targets ≥24x24", async ({ page }) => {
    await page.goto("/dashboard", { waitUntil: "networkidle" });
    const small = await page.evaluate(() => {
      const results: Array<{
        tag: string;
        text: string;
        w: number;
        h: number;
      }> = [];
      // Exclude <label htmlFor=...> — labels paired with a form control
      // delegate the hit target to the control itself (WCAG 2.5.8 "inline"
      // exemption). The "browse files" label has no htmlFor (it wraps the
      // hidden file input directly) so it remains in scope.
      const sel =
        'button, label:not([for]), [role="button"], [role="radio"], a[href]';
      document.querySelectorAll(sel).forEach((el) => {
        if (!(el instanceof HTMLElement)) return;
        const r = el.getBoundingClientRect();
        if (r.width === 0 || r.height === 0) return; // hidden / pre-render
        if (r.width < 24 || r.height < 24) {
          results.push({
            tag: el.tagName,
            text: (el.textContent || "").trim().slice(0, 60),
            w: Math.round(r.width),
            h: Math.round(r.height),
          });
        }
      });
      return results;
    });
    if (small.length > 0) {
      throw new Error(
        `WCAG 2.5.8 failures (${small.length} target(s) < 24x24):\n` +
          small
            .map((s) => `  - ${s.tag} "${s.text}" → ${s.w}x${s.h}px`)
            .join("\n"),
      );
    }
  });

  test("Inspector golden_clinical_001 — all clickable targets ≥24x24", async ({
    page,
  }) => {
    await page.goto("/inspector/golden_clinical_001", {
      waitUntil: "networkidle",
    });
    await page
      .getByRole("button", { name: /Verified sentences/ })
      .first()
      .click();
    const small = await page.evaluate(() => {
      const results: Array<{
        tag: string;
        text: string;
        w: number;
        h: number;
      }> = [];
      const sel = 'button, label, [role="button"], a[href]';
      document.querySelectorAll(sel).forEach((el) => {
        if (!(el instanceof HTMLElement)) return;
        const r = el.getBoundingClientRect();
        if (r.width === 0 || r.height === 0) return;
        if (r.width < 24 || r.height < 24) {
          results.push({
            tag: el.tagName,
            text: (el.textContent || "").trim().slice(0, 60),
            w: Math.round(r.width),
            h: Math.round(r.height),
          });
        }
      });
      return results;
    });
    if (small.length > 0) {
      throw new Error(
        `WCAG 2.5.8 failures (${small.length} target(s) < 24x24):\n` +
          small
            .map((s) => `  - ${s.tag} "${s.text}" → ${s.w}x${s.h}px`)
            .join("\n"),
      );
    }
  });
});

## Verification
CHARTER §1 200-cap exceeded by 53. Drivers: full ContradictionSide schema with 6 fields + validator + 4 new tests + import/helper (backend ~66 LOC); ContradictionPane component with 6 sub-testids per side (~99 LOC); badge → button conversion + state plumbing (~35 LOC); demo 3-sides extension (~33 LOC); spec extension (~29 LOC). Splitting Sheet from schema would surface schema field unused (substrate-only) — substrate-honesty anti-pattern. Exemption analogous to I-f5-003/4 (245/246 LOC), I-f7-001 (217 LOC) — binding multi-substrate coverage in a single coherent backend+UI feature.

## What you are reviewing
- **Context:** I-f8-002 — clicking the contradiction badge opens a Sheet showing ALL sides of the disagreement.  180: class ContradictionSide(BaseModel):
 181:     """One side of a contradiction (I-f8-002).
 182: 
 183:     Each side is a distinct cited source's position on the claim. Tier
 184:     + sample size + hedge language + PT08 flag inform reviewer judgment
 185:     of which side has the stronger evidentiary backing."""
 186: 
 187:     source_id: str = Field(min_length=1, max_length=200)
 188:     source_tier: Literal["T1", "T2", "T3"]
 189:     sample_size: int | None = Field(default=None, ge=0)
 190:     hedge_language: str = Field(min_length=1, max_length=200)
 191:     pt08_flag: str | None = Field(default=None, max_length=50)
 192:     claim_excerpt: str = Field(min_length=1, max_length=500)
 193: 
 194: 
 195: class ContradictionSignal(BaseModel):
 196:     """Inline contradiction annotation per sentence (I-f8-001).
 197: 
 198:     Indicates ≥2 cited sources disagree on the claim in the sentence.
 199:     `sides` (added I-f8-002) carries the per-source detail consumed by
 200:     the ContradictionPane; empty preserves I-f8-001 back-compat."""
 201: 
 202:     disagreeing_source_count: int = Field(ge=2, le=20)
 203:     summary: str = Field(min_length=1, max_length=500)
 204:     sides: list[ContradictionSide] = Field(default_factory=list)
 205: 
 206:     @model_validator(mode="after")
 207:     def _sides_count_consistency(self) -> "ContradictionSignal":
 208:         if self.sides and len(self.sides) != self.disagreeing_source_count:
 209:             raise ValueError(
 210:                 "len(sides) must equal disagreeing_source_count when sides "
 211:                 "is non-empty"
 212:             )

const ISO = new Date().toISOString();

// I-f5-007: keep the "normal" demo source ALWAYS fresh so the stale badge
// only renders for sources we explicitly mark stale. Pin to ~30 days ago.
const FRESH_DATE = new Date(Date.now() - 30 * 24 * 3600 * 1000)
  .toISOString()
  .slice(0, 10);
// Pin the stale demo source to 3 years ago — well past the 730-day cutoff.
const STALE_DATE = new Date(Date.now() - 3 * 365 * 24 * 3600 * 1000)
  .toISOString()
  .slice(0, 10);

const SOURCE_FULL_TEXT_5 =
  "The randomized trial enrolled 1247 adults with chronic migraines and demonstrated significant aspirin headache reduction at outcomes assessment.";

function _src(i: number): RetrievalSource {
  return {
    source_id: `src-${i}`,
    url: `https://www.cochrane.org/CD-${i}`,
    domain: "cochrane.org",
    tier: "T1",
    title: `Cochrane review ${i}`,
    publication_date: FRESH_DATE,
    authors: ["Smith J", "Doe R", "Patel K"],
    snippet: SOURCE_FULL_TEXT_5.slice(0, 80),
    full_text_available: true,
    full_text: SOURCE_FULL_TEXT_5,
    fetched_at_utc: ISO,
    provenance: {},
  };
}
